coredata.dc:description: The development of innovative methods for waste plastic valorization can benefit the environment and economy. In this study, the full spectrum of products (gas, oil, and solid products) resulting from the pyrolysis–reforming of a plastic mixture functioned as precursors of value-added products: 1) ethylene–propylene copolymers, 2) polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and 3) carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CNFs were subsequently applied as supercapacitor electrodes, whereasthe CQDs acted as co-sensitizers for the primary sensitizer, N3, in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Adding oil to PAN increased the specific capacitance of the CNFs by 46.12% to 226.64F/g, compared with that of pure PAN CNFs. With the aid of CQDs, the short-circuit current density and photo-conversion efficiency of DSSCs increased by 25.89% and 17.08%, respectively. Mechanisms associated with the integrated valorization pathway were proposed.

head.ce:title: Transformative and sustainable approach to waste plastic mixture valorization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the proposed integrated method (The cited references are shown in Supplementary information, “3 References of Fig. 1”). This graphical abstract is also the Fig. 1 of the manuscript.

The development of innovative methods for waste plastic valorization can benefit the environment and economy. In this study, the full spectrum of products (gas, oil, and solid products) resulting from the pyrolysis–reforming of a plastic mixture functioned as precursors of value-added products: 1) ethylene–propylene copolymers, 2) polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and 3) carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CNFs were subsequently applied as supercapacitor electrodes, whereasthe CQDs acted as co-sensitizers for the primary sensitizer, N3, in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Adding oil to PAN increased the specific capacitance of the CNFs by 46.12% to 226.64F/g, compared with that of pure PAN CNFs. With the aid of CQDs, the short-circuit current density and photo-conversion efficiency of DSSCs increased by 25.89% and 17.08%, respectively. Mechanisms associated with the integrated valorization pathway were proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics have become ubiquitous due to their low price, durability, and moldability. Plastics include different types, such as polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), polystyrene (PS), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). Annual global plastic production increased from 2 million tons in 1950 to 460 million tons in 2019 [1]. In 2019, the amount of waste plastics generated worldwide was 353 million tons; 49 % and 19 % were landfilled and incinerated respectively, only 16 % were collected for recycling, and 23 % were mismanaged [2]. These disposal methods are unsustainable. Landfilled and mismanaged plastics accumulate in the ecosystem, and the plastic particles, additives, and pathogens enter the food chain. Incineration cannot produce sufficient energy to compensate for the costs and releases carbon dioxide and other pollutants [3]. The current recycling methods are mainly mechanical reprocessing. Although they avoid losing plastic materials, the disadvantages are sorting limitations, contamination, and deteriorating quality [3]. To address these problems, valorization has emerged as a promising method, it converts waste plastics into value-added products and mitigates pollution [4,5]. Zhang et al. [6] adopted photoelectrochemical technology to valorize PET. Formate and hydrogen were produced from the photoanode and cathode respectively. Zhao et al. [7] dehydrogenated plastics and produced value-added carbon nanotubes. The process was powered by microwave and assisted by self-dispersing monometallic and bimetallic catalysts. Williams et al. [8] integrated alkaline thermal treatment and electrochemical process. This process called elecATT converted the mixture of LDPE and seaweeds into value-added products: hydrogen (H2), waxy polymeric condensates, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). The application of nickel supported by zirconia and zeolite further enhanced the production of H2. Cao et al. [9] used HCl from PVC dehydrochlorination to attack PET, converting PVC and PET into terephthalic acid and 1,2-dichloroethane. Kots et al. [10] dechlorinated PVC, making metal-based catalysis possible during the subsequent hydrogenolysis to produce lubricants. Sun et al. [11] catalytically pyrolyzed polyesters to produce methyl methacrylate, which avoids biodegradation that causes CO2 emissions. Wyss et al. [12] carbonized vehicle waste plastic into graphene via fast discharge. The above methods decomposed plastic molecules and obtained gas, liquid or solid as value-added products. However, these products are feedstocks of the next phase, and few studies have explored their applications.

In this work, we integrated different valorization methods to simultaneously utilize gas, liquid, and solid products. To break down plastic molecules, we chose pyrolysis-reforming. Pyrolysis is simple and mature [13], it produces intermediate volatiles and solid residue. The intermediate volatiles enter the reforming section, by controlling the reforming temperature, the compositions of gas and liquid can be tuned. The products from pyrolysis-reforming also depend on plastic mixture (PM). PE and PP lead to ethylene (C2H4), propylene (C3H6), and other aliphatics; [13,14] PVC and PS result in aromatics [13]. This inspired us to valorize PMs into the following value-added products: ethylene–propylene (EP) copolymers, carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs); a schematic is shown in Fig. 1 . First, C2H4 and C3H6 can be used to synthesize EP copolymers that can result in industrially important materials such as EP elastomers and EP impact-strength modifiers [15]. EP copolymers are also precursors of EP rubber, which has broad applications in the automobile industry, especially in tires and O-rings. Second, CNFs possess good physical and chemical properties, such as electrical conductivity and chemical stability. These features enable various engineering applications, including adsorption, sensors, and catalysts [16]. Second, the liquid products can be added to the precursor of CNFs. During heat treatment, they function as porogens which vaporize and create pore spaces [17]. Meanwhile, the aromatics in the oil become graphite, it supplies carbon materials and increases conductivity [18]. Therefore we modified CNFs using liquid products and applied the CNFs as supercapacitor electrodes. Third, quantum dots are a series of nanosized crystalline semiconductors [19]. CQDs are a class of quantum dots that exhibit biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent fluorescent properties [20]. The high carbon content of the solid product of PMs makes it a possible precursor of CQDs. Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) is a third-generation photovoltaic cell with multiple advantages [21]. To improve the affordability, stability and efficiency, technologies such as porous carbon electrode [22], polymer gel electrolytes [21] and different co-sensitizers have been proposed. Due to their optical properties, CQDs can function as co-sensitizers [23–25]. In this work we examined how CQDs co-sensitize cis-bis (isothiocyanato) bis (2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) (N3) dye, of which publications are limited.

In summary, this study presents an integrated process for valorizing PMs. PM was first decomposed by pyrolysis–reforming; the products served as precursors of EP copolymers, CNFs, and CQDs (EP–CNF–CQDs). The CNFs were fabricated into supercapacitor electrodes, and the CQDs functioned as co-sensitizers of DSSCs. Due to pyrolysis–reforming products, the performances of both applications were improved, and the possible mechanisms were discussed.

The composition of the PM was adapted from statistics based on a document from Department of Energy (DE-FOA-0002376), which provides a typical plastic-mixture recipe for studying waste recycling. The mixture was composed of virgin plastics purchased from Sigma Aldrich: 32 % high-density polyethylene, 32 % low-density polyethylene, 11 % PVC, 4 % PP, and 21 % PS (by weight).

The pyrolysis–reforming setup is shown in Fig. S1. The pyrolysis–reforming reaction occurred in a quartz tube (110 cm long, 25 mm outer diameter and 2.5 mm wall thickness), under 50.83 sccm ultra-high-purity N2 gas. The quartz tube was loaded into two identical tube furnaces (Carbolite Gero) connected in series; the first functioned as the pyrolysis section, and the second served as the reforming section. Before the experiment, 3 g PM was placed in an alumina boat that was in the middle of pyrolysis section, the air in the quartz tube was purged by N2, and reforming section was preheated at one of the reforming temperatures (500, 550, 600, 650, and 700 °C). During the experiment, the pyrolysis section ramped from room temperature to 500°C at 10 °C/min and held for 80 min. Due to the variation of room temperature, the gas bag was not connected to the outlet until pyrolysis section reached 100 °C, making one experiment 120 min. The liquid product condensed at outlet of the quartz tube and collection bottles. The solid product remained in the alumina boat. The neutralization bottle removed hydrogen chloride (HCl) by 200 mL 1 M NaOH; the desiccant was anhydrous Copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4). A buffer test tube was used to prevent the mixture of the condensed products and NaOH solution. Gas products were studied on gas chromatography system (GC), liquid product was studied on a GC–mass spectroscopy and ATR spectrometer, and solid product was studied by using an elemental analyzer, XRD, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). HCl was quantified by performing ion chromatography.

During the experiment, the non-condensable entered a Restek Multi-Layer Foil 12 L bag after passing the neutralization bottle and desiccant test tube. After the experiment, quartz tubes, tubings, and condensing bottles were washed with GCMS-grade acetone (Sigma Aldrich). The solution was filtered by filter paper (Whatman 25 mm diameter paper, with 11 μm pore size). The filtrate was placed on a hot plate at 60 °C to remove the acetone and keep the solute. The non-condensable in the bag, the filtrate solute, the filter paper deposit, and the alumina boat residue were called gas, oil, wax, and solid, respectively. Oil and wax constitute liquid products.

To determine the range of temperature at which degradation occurs, TGA and differential thermogravimetry (DTG) were performed on a TA Instruments Q600 SDT analyzer. Under the N2 atmosphere, the temperature ramped from room temperature (25 ℃) to 700 ℃ at 10 ℃/min and stayed isothermal for 2 h. The gas component was identified on an SRI8610C GC with multigas #3 configuration. The GC was equipped with a Restek 6′ Haysep D column, a Restek 6′ 13X molecular sieve column (6 feet × 2 mm ID), and a 30 m Agilent HP-AL/S column (30 m × 0.530 mm × 15 μm). The 200 mL 1 M NaOH was analyzed on a Thermo Scientific ICS 5000 equipped with AS23 columns and a self-regenerating suppressor. Oil was analyzed using Agilent gas chromatography/mass spectroscopy (GC/MS, 7890A/5975C). The column was an Agilent HP-5MS UI, 30 m × 0.250 mm, with 0.25 μm coating. Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) spectra of oil and wax were acquired on a ThermoFisher iS50 ATR, the number of scans was 64, the resolution was 4 cm−1, and the wavelength ranged from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The morphology of solid was visualized on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250, FEI). XRD patterns were measured on a Rigaku Smart-lab X-ray diffraction system, the scanning range was from 10° to 90° at a rate of 4°/min, and chopper increment of 0.02°. The elemental composition was measured on a Vario Macro cube CHNOS elemental analyzer.

EP copolymer synthesis requires a large volume of C2H4 and C3H6 which cannot be produced on this lab-scale pyrolysis-reforming setup. Therefore, cylindrical gases were used to simulate this process. EP molar ratios of the feed gas were determined from the GC results. The catalyst and co-catalyst were vanadium oxychloride (VOCl3, Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc.) and ethylaluminum sesquichloride ((C2H5)3Al2Cl3; EASC; Aldrich Chemistry, 97 % purity). Ethyl trichloroacetate (ETCA, Aldrich, 97 % purity) was added as a promoter to keep the catalyst active [26]. The synthesis occurred in an Ace Glass one-piece 500-ml pressure reactor, rated 45 psi at 100 °C; a pressure relief valve was set at 30 psig. The experiment was performed at 25 °C. 300 mL of hexane was added to the reactor and the stirrer was maintained at 750 RPM. After the air in the reactor was replaced by N2, sequentially injected 3.55 mmol VOCl3, 17.75 mmol ETCA, and 750 mmol EASC. Before polymerization, stop N2 supply, introduce the feed (C2H4 and C3H6 mixture) to purge N2, and the synthesis started by shutting outlet valve of the reactor. The total feed flow rate was maintained at 400 sccm for 30 min. The substance in the reactor was poured into a beaker, 300 ml of ethanol was added as the terminating agent, this mixture was stirred by a glass rod for 1 min. After standing for 5 min, EP copolymer appeared as a white lump which was subsequently dried in a convection oven (Lab Companion) at 70 °C overnight.

To analyze the monomer sequence and ratios, NMR spectra were measured. 1 g EP copolymer was dissolved in 10 mL o-dichlorobenzene (ODCB) at 180 °C, making a 10 % (g/mL) solution. It was subsequently loaded in a 10 mm OD NMR sample tube. 13C NMR spectra were measured by a Bruker Avance III 600 NMR spectrometer, operating at Larmor frequencies of 150.9 MHz (13C) at 125 °C. The assignment of the NMR spectral peaks was based on previous studies [27,28], shown in Table S3, and the monomers were quantified using equations (1) – (4) [27,28].

Here, P and S denote the peak area of the primary and secondary carbon atoms, respectively. N0 and N1 are intermediary quantities. C2H4mol% and C3H6mol% are the molar percentages of C2H4 and C3H6, respectively. The molecular parameters of the polymer were calculated by applying equations (5) and (6).

Where M is the average molecular weight of the EP copolymer (g/mol), Mi is the molecular weight of the chain, ni is the number of chains with molecular weight Mi, and k is a non-negative integer. When k = 0, M is the number-average molecular weight, denoted as Mn; when k = 1, M is the weight-average molecular weight, denoted as Mw. PDI is the polydispersity index that measures the breadth of the molar mass distribution.

High-temperature gel permeation chromatograph (HT GPC) was performed by PolyAnalytic company to analyze the molecular weights. For each sample, 200 mg EP copolymer was cut into fragments and transferred into a wire mesh, and 5 mL 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene (TCB) was added and heated at 150 °C for 1 h to dissolve the EP copolymer fully. Approximately 1 mL of the solution was diluted in a 5 mL mobile phase, which was also TCB. The diluted solution was heated for a minimum 1 h at 150 °C then injected into a Tosoh HLC8321 GPC/HT System instrument.

CNFs were fabricated by electrospinning owing to the simplicity and flexibility. In the control group, the precursor was 1.2 g of PAN (Sigma-Aldrich, average Mw 150,000, CAS No. 25014–41–9). In the experimental group, the precursors were prepared by replacing 25 % PAN by oil of the same weight, i.e., 0.9 g PAN plus 0.3 g oil. The precursor was dissolved in 10 ml DMF (Sigma-Aldrich, biotech grade ≥ 99.9 %, CAS No. 68–12–2) by stirring at 90 °C for 12 h. The solutions were loaded into a Luerlock 20-ml syringe with a 24-gauge needle (ID: 1.194 mm). The syringe was placed horizontally, and the needle tip was 90 mm away from the cylindrical collector wrapped in tin foil. During the spinning, the syringe pump was maintained at 0.8 mL/h, voltage was tuned between 7 and 10 kV to maintain the Taylor cone, and collector rotated at 30 RPM. The spinning time was 10 h. The collected fibers were referred to as fresh CNFs (FCNFs). To obtain CNFs, FCNFs were heat-treated in a tube furnace (Thermo Scientific Lindberg/Blue M). During stabilization, the temperature was ramped at 1 °C/min to 300 °C and maintained for 1 h under 70 sccm air. After cooling to 25 °C, carbonization followed by ramping at 10 °C/min to 800 °C and maintained for 3 h under 70 sccm N2. The acquired CNFs were in the form of mat.

The CNFs were visualized on SEM (Quanta 250, FEI). To study their pore structures, N2 isothermal adsorption/desorption data were collected on a Micromeritics ASAP 2000 adsorption analyzer. The specific surface area (SSA) was calculated based on the type of the isotherms, and the total pore volume (Vtp) was determined by the adsorption amount when relative pressure, p/p0, equals 0.99, where p is the equilibrium pressure at adsorption temperature, 77 K (−196.15 ℃), and p0 is the saturation pressure of N2 at the same temperature. The pore width (pore size) distribution was analyzed by ASAP 2020 software built-in “N2 @ 77 on Carbon Slit Pores by non-local density functional theory (NLDFT)” method.

Supercapacitor electrodes were fabricated using CNFs. One electrode was a 1 cm by 1 cm CNFs wrapped in nickel foam; the other was a 2.5 cm by 2.5 cm, 1 mm thick platinum foil. Their electrochemical properties were measured by a three-electrode system, soaked in 6 M KOH. The CNFs, a platinum foil, and an Hg/HgO electrode (Hg/HgO, CHI instrument, CHI 152) functioned as working, counter, and reference electrodes (WE, CE, and RE) respectively. The test was controlled by a CHI6203D electrochemical analyzer. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanned at 0.005 V/s, the potential ranged from 0 to −0.8 V to avoid electrolyzing the KOH solution; Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves were measured at a constant specific current of 1 A/g (A per unit weight of CNF), the high and low potential was 0 and −0.8 V; Stability (capacitance retention) of the CNFs were tested by 450 such GCD cycles; electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were acquired in a frequency ranging from 10,000 to 0.01 Hz, the amplitude was 0.005 V. The specific capacitance was calculated by equation (7) and GCD results.
(7)
C
GCD

I s

Here, CGCD are specific capacitances calculated from GCD curves, (F/g); Is is the specific current (A/g); t is the time elapsed since the start of the test (s); V is the voltage between the working and reference electrodes (V); t1 and t2 are the beginning and ending time of a discharge segment, respectively (s).

Hydrothermal approach is facile and nontoxic; therefore, it was adopted as the preparation technique. First, 0.8 g solid was ground in a mortar, mixed with 20 mL of 5 vol% H2O2 in a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and sealed. Next, a convection oven (Lab Companion) was preheated to 180 °C, and the autoclave was treated for 6 h to obtain the CQD solution. The solution was vacuum-filtered through a 0.45-μm Fluropore membrane filter having a diameter of 47 mm. Additional deionized (DI) water was used to thoroughly wash the glassware and autoclave. Finally, 80 mL of the CQD solution was obtained. Meanwhile, the photoanodes were based on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glasses (Sigma Aldrich). FTO glasses were cut into 1.5 cm by 2 cm, and TiO2 paste was deposited by the Doctor’s blade method. Scotch tape (overall thickness 2.3 mil) with a punched ¼’’ (6.35 mm) hole served as a mask. The glasses were dried at 120 ℃ for 10 min, then heated at 325 ℃ for 5 min, at 375 ℃ for 5 min, at 450 ℃ for 15 min, and finally at 500 ℃ for 1 h. This calcination method was adapted from Ji et al. [29]. A transparent TiO2 paste (Solaronix, part No. 14411) was deposited and heat-treated, then a diffusive paste was deposited by the same procedure. The diffusive paste was prepared by mixing 2 g TiO2 anatase powder, 0.4 g ethyl cellulose and 8 mL terpineol and stirring overnight. 40 uL CQDs solution was spin-coated onto TiO2 paste and dried at 90 ℃; the glasses were soaked in 1 mM N 3 (Sigma Aldrich) for 24 h, and acquired products were photoanodes. To assemble the DSSC, a photoanode and a 1.5 cm × 2.0 cm Pt-coated FTO glass (cathode) sandwiched a 3-mil spacer (1.5 cm by 1 cm Kapton polyimide film) with a hole (9/32′’ diameter, i.e., 7.14 mm), two binder clips held the electrodes. The electrolyte components were modified from previous publications [24,25,30]. shown in Table S6. The detailed architecture of the DSSCs is demonstrated in Fig. S14.

The CQDs particles were visualized by transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F) operated at 200 kV. The CQDs solution was irradiated by an ultraviolet flashlight (Alonefire SV003, wavelength 365 nm, power 10 W) for a preliminary observation since CQDs would emit fluorescence if excited by ultraviolet. To study the dependence between exciting and emitting wavelengths of CQDs, fluorescent light (FL) spectra contour map (FL map) was acquired on a Horiba Fluorolog fluorometer. The exciting wavelengths were scanned from 200 to 600 nm, with an interval of 4 nm. The emission light was scanned from 294 to 700 nm with an interval of 2 nm. The ultraviolet–visible light spectroscopy (UV–vis) was studied on a SpectraMax M2 Ultraviolet–visible spectrometer; the incident wavelengths were from 200 to 900 nm, and the sampling interval was 1 nm; the result is called UV–vis in this work.

To examine the electrochemical properties, an LSH-7520 LED solar lamp irradiated the solar cells, the irradiance spectrum was Air mass 1.5 Global (AM1.5 G), and the power intensity was 100 mW/cm2. Linear sweep voltammetry data were obtained on a CHI6203D electrochemical analyzer, the scan was from −1 to 1 V, at 0.02 V/s. Photo-conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of the DSSCs were calculated from equations (8) – (12).

Here, P is the power intensity of the DSSC (mW/cm2); V is the voltage between the working and reference electrodes (V); J is the current density (mA/cm2) at V; I is the measured current (mA) at V; A is the size of the active area (cm2); FF is the fill factor; Pmax is the maximum of P; JSC is the short-circuit current density (mA/cm2); VOC is the open circuit voltage (V); PCE is the photo-conversion efficiency; and Pin is the incident-light power intensity (mW/cm2).

To better understand the performances of DSSCs, external quantum efficiency (EQE) was obtained on an MKS QuantX300 quantum efficiency measurement system. Besides JSC from J-V curve, another JSC denoted as JSC(EQE) was calculated by equation (13). In this work, JSC refers to the value from J-V curve if not specified. JSC and JSC(EQE) can validate mutually.

Where λmin and λmax are the shortest and longest wavelength of the solar lamp light (nm); λ is the wavelength of light (nm); Ee,λ is the spectral irradiance generated by the solar lamp (W/cm2/nm); h is plank constant, 6.626 × 10-34 J/Hz; ν is the frequency of the light (Hz) at a wavelength, ν = speed of light/λ; e is the charge of one electron −1.6 × 10-19C; the minus sign “-” converts JSC(EQE) to positive value.

The EIS was measured under AM1.5 G on CHI6230D electrochemical analyzer, the frequency ranged from 0.01 to 1 M Hz, and the alternating voltage amplitude was 10 mV. Nyquist and Bode plots were extracted from EIS data. Nyquist plot was fitted on EC-Lab V10.40 software. The electron lifetime was calculated by equation (14).

Where τ is the lifetime (ms), and fmax is the characteristic frequency (Hz) on the Bode plot.

The HOMO and LUMO of the CQDs were evaluated by CV. The method was based on previous studies [24,25,31]. The working electrode was prepared by dropping the CQD solution on a Pt-coated FTO glass and drying at 90 ℃; the counter electrode was a 25 mm × 25 mm Pt sheet with thickness of 1 mm. The reference electrode was Ag/AgCl, and the electrolyte was 0.1 mM tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile. CV curves were acquired on a CHI6203D electrochemical analyzer scanned from −1.5 to 1.5 V at 50 mV/s. When the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl, the HOMO and LUMO were calculated by Equations (15) and (16), respectively. The energy levels of the FTO coating, TiO2 substrate, I-/I3- redox, and Pt coating were found in a previous work [24]. The HOMO and LUMO of N3 were found on the Ossila website [32].

Here, EHOMO and ELUMO are the energy level of the HOMO and LUMO (eV), respectively; Eox vs. Al-AgCl and Ered vs. Ag-AgCl are the oxidation and reduction potentials vs. Ag/AgCl (V), respectively.

The thermal behavior of the PM was studied by TGA, the results are shown in Fig. S2. When temperature was higher than 497 ℃, weight loss became negligible, meaning the PM was completely pyrolyzed. In this study, pyrolysis–reforming produced 1) hydrocarbon gas and hydrogen (H2), 2) liquids including wax and oil, 3) solids remaining in the pyrolysis section, and 4) hydrogen chloride (HCl). Their yields are summarized in Fig. 2 a and Table S1. At each reforming temperature, the sums of the yields were all above 93 %, achieving mass balance. The weight loss was due to evaporation of the oil and wax remaining in the quartz tube. As the reforming temperature increased, the gas yield increased from 7.04 % at 500 ℃ to 48.67 % at 700 ℃. Meanwhile, the oil yield decreased from 45.06 % to 27.08 %. Between 650 and 700 ℃, the wax yield increased from a minimum of 8.17 % to 10.65 %; this was caused by more extensive carbonization and coking (we refer to it as “wax” for simplicity). Because the pyrolysis section always stayed at 500 °C, the solid and HCl yields changed marginally. Fig. 2b shows the C2H4 and C3H6 yields at different reforming temperatures, detailed information of gas composition can be found in Table S2 and Fig. S3. From 500 to 650 ℃, these yields increased. At 700 ℃, the yield of C3H6 dropped. In the meantime, the C2H4 to C3H6 (EP) molar ratio increased slowly from 1.14 to 1.42, and at 700 ℃, it increased to 1.91. This was because at 700 ℃, more C3+ was decomposed to C2H4 and smaller molecules. The carbon number distribution of oil is shown in Fig. 2c. The oil included aliphatics and aromatics; the latter was monoaromatic hydrocarbons (MAHs), such as benzene, ethylbenzene, and indene, and polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), such as naphthalene and methylnaphthalene. The C16+ products were long-chain aliphatics, C11-15 contained aliphatics and MAHs, and C6-10 contained mainly MAHs and PAHs. The number of carbons reduced as higher temperatures favored bond breaking and aromatization. Distribution of oil aliphatics and aromatics, and functional groups of oil and wax are shown in Fig. S4 and S5, respectively. Fig. S5b suggests that the wax contains mainly long-chain aliphatics at 500–600 ℃. Functional groups almost disappear at 650–700 ℃ because high temperatures decomposed most of the hydrocarbons and the wax is carbonized. Fig. 2d shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the solid. Three peaks appear at diffraction angles (2θ) of 25.00°, 43.25°, and 79.89°. These are identified as the 002, 100, and 112 planes of turbostratic carbon black [33,34] Elemental analysis reveals that the solid consisted of 96.72 % carbon and 3.28 % hydrogen by weight. The morphology of the solid is visualized on an electron scanning microscope (SEM) shown in Fig. S6. A detailed discussion of the possible mechanisms of pyrolysis–reforming is provided in Supplementary Information.

The C2H4 content of commercial EP copolymers varies widely depending on the application. Three EP copolymers were prepared based on the EP molar ratio reformed at 500, 600, and 700 ℃, they were denoted as EP-500, −600, and −700, respectively. The EP molar ratios in the feed were 1.14, 1.33, and 1.91, respectively. Their photos can be seen in Fig. 3 a, and NMR spectra are shown in Fig. 3b. Compared to NMR spectra of other EP copolymers [27,35] the peaks on our spectra are located at the same chemical shifts. The peak assignment is shown in Table S3. The EP molar ratios of the copolymers were 1.11, 1.39, and 2.02, which were close to their corresponding feed compositions. For demonstration purposes, high-temperature gel permeation chromatography (HT GPC) analysis was performed on EP-500 only. The number-average molecular weight (Mn) and weight average molecular weight (Mw) were 44,507 and 129,751 g/mol, respectively, and PDI was 2.92. The Mw is no higher than 30 % of EP copolymers and EP-diene monomers (EPDMs) synthesized in other published works [35, [36]. Molecular weight can be increased by extending reaction time, tuning the amount of catalyst, and increasing the feed gas pressure. Our PDI value indicates a uniform molecular weight distribution and is close to previous works [35–37].

In this study, only oil was adopted as precursors of CNFs. Wax was not utilized because it precipitated in DMF solution at room temperature. Fortunately, wax by itself is a value-added product, it can serve as intermediate for fuel and other chemicals [8,38] Four samples were prepared, their precursors: 1) 100 % PAN and 75 % PAN plus 25 % oil reformed at 2) 500, 3) 600, and 4) 700 °C. They were labeled as CNF-100PAN, CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700, respectively. The yield after heat treatment was listed in Table S4, weight loss was large due to the loss of residual DMF, nitrogen in PAN, hydrogen and oil. SEM images of the samples are presented in Fig. 4 , the fibers were smooth, and their diameters ranged from 150 to 400 nm. Elemental spectra are shown in Fig. S7, carbon was the only detected element. Since the samples were nanosized fibers, and the composition was carbon, they consisted solely of CNFs.

Isotherms and pore size distributions of CNFs can be seen in Fig. S8 and S9. The isotherms were type I, and the adsorption was governed by micropores [39]. Therefore, Langmuir rather than Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) model should be used due to its monolayer adsorption. The specific surface area (SSA) and total pore volume (Vtp) are summarized in Table 1 . The SSA and Vtp of CNF-100PAN were 360.49 m2/g and 0.15 cm3/g, respectively, and those of CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700 were higher. This was because during heat treatment, the long-chain aliphatics in the oil decomposed and vaporized, generating pore spaces [40]. CNF-25Oil-500 had the highest SSA and Vtp of 566.15 m2/g and 0.23 cm3/g, respectively; however, as the oil was reformed at 600 and 700 ℃, the SSA and Vtp dropped. This was because the oil contained fewer long-chain hydrocarbons; thus the porogenic effect was weaker.

CNFs were tested as the working electrodes of supercapacitors, and the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves are shown in Fig. 5 a and b. CV curves resemble rectangles, which is a typical capacitive feature. According to equation (7), the CGCD of CNF-100PAN was 155.10F/g, and that of CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700 were 226.64, 184.58, and 184.11F/g. Compared to published results (see Table S5), although our CGCDs were moderate, oil increased the CGCD by as high as 46.12 %, proving its effectiveness. To achieve higher specific capacitance, the amount of oil can be tuned for more pore structures, and/or electroactive agents can be introduced to create pseudocapacitance [41]. The Nyquist plot is shown in Fig. 5c; the intercept with Z’ axis represents electrolyte resistance. The semicircle is related to charge-transfer resistances (Rct) at the CNFs/electrolyte interface. The semicircles are followed by a line at 45° caused by Warburg impedance, and the near vertical lines in the low-frequency region show capacitive property and leakage resistance [41,42]. Our CNFs were electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) electrodes, because they did not contain electroactive cites (e.g. metal oxides, conductive polymers, etc) [42], and no redox peak was found on CV curves. EDLCs store charge by electrostatic process, thus the capacitance depends on high accessible SSA. As can be seen in Table 1, the higher the SSA, the higher the CGCD. The accessibility of the SSA to charge can be studied by Warburg coefficient, σ (Ω/s0.5). To obtain it, ω-0.5 (angular frequency) was fitted linearly to Z’, and σ was equivalent to the slope [41]. The ω was at Warburg diffusion region shown in Fig. 5c. A low σ suggests facile mass transport of charge from electrolyte to electrode surface. The σ of CNF-100PAN, CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700 were 2.76, 1.92, 2.06 and 2.63 Ω/s0.5. The lower σ was due to more pore structure as shown in Table 1. Rct values of CNF-100PAN, CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700 were 2.23, 1.01, 1.30 and 1.97 Ω, respectively. The lower Rct was because the aromatics in the oil were carbonized, and resultant graphite enhanced conductivity [17,43,44]. The stabilities of the samples are depicted in Fig. 5d. After approximately 100 cycles, the capacitance retention of all samples stabilized. At the end, CNF-100PAN retained 91.45 %, and CNF-25Oil-500, −600, and −700 retained 94.15 %, 97.85 %, and 96.78 %, respectively.

Possible mechanisms of heat treatment were revealed by attenuated total reflection (ATR) spectra shown in Fig. S10, and Fig. S11 shows the process. Stabilization converted PAN into a ladder structure. The cyclization bonded the carbon atom of the nitrile group (–C≡N) to the nitrogen atom of a neighboring nitrile group. Thus, the nitrile group formed a double bond (C = N). Although cyclization could occur in an inert atmosphere, oxygen 1) served as an initiator and inhibitor of cyclization [45], 2) caused dehydrogenation [46] and 3) produced functional groups, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl [46,47] and ether, as indicated in Fig. S11b. Compared with inert stabilization, oxidative stabilization increases the thermal stability of CNFs [48] and provides another crosslinking path (intermolecular elimination of water) during carbonization [47]. In the carbonization phase, the ladder molecules are combined by eliminating water, nitrogen, and hydrogen. The oxidation of oil is also a complicated process. First, a hydrocarbon molecule is initialized to form a radical that absorbs oxygen and forms a peroxyl radical. Hydroperoxyl groups are produced via hydrogen abstraction. Hydroperoxide is unstable; it may eject a hydroxyl and abstract another hydrogen atom to form a new hydroxyl [49,50] or lose water to become a carbonyl group [51]. Subsequent reactions produce carboxylic acids and esters [52]. If two hydroxyls substitute aromatic hydrogens to produce dihydroxybenzene, more oxygen would be required to form bridged species and decompose to maleic acid and acetylene [53]. Aromatics may also crosslink with esters or diacetyl peroxides [54]. Carbonization decomposed oxidized oil, which cracked CNFs when vaporizing, creating surface areas and channels; it also polymerizes aromatics to graphite that reduced Rct. The oxidation of PAN and oil involves radicals, they may interact and create structural defects, leading to extra pore spaces. In summary, applying oil created more paths from the electrolyte to the electrode surface and adsorption area, reduced charge-transfer resistance, and strengthened stability.

A 365-nm ultraviolet (UV) flashlight-irradiated CQD solution and the fluorescence emission are shown in Fig. 6 a. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the CQDs are presented in Fig. 6b. The CQDs are spherical and monodispersed in the solution. The diameter ranges from 3.84 to 6.90 nm, with an average size of 5.20 nm and a standard deviation of 0.95 nm; the size distribution is displayed in Fig. S12. The FL map of the CQD solution shown in Fig. 6a indicate that the emission wavelength is highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is 290–365 nm, the emission wavelength ranges from 372 to 510 nm. An excitation wavelength of 327 nm produces the strongest emission at 438 nm. The FL map also shows at every excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength is always larger, indicating a redshift. Three absorption peaks of N3 and the CQDs are identified in the UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra shown in Fig. 6b. The N3 spectrum has peaks at 249, 308, 375, and 504 nm. The spectrum of CQDs has three peaks. The one at 262 nm was attributed to the excitation of π → π* of the aromatic C = C bond [55]. The broad peak at 290–311 nm was due to the n → π* transition of the C = O bond [56,57]. A broad tail extends to the visible range (400–600 nm), and a tiny peak appears at 500 nm, possibly due to other functional groups.

Different labels are assigned to solar cells. SC-TiO2 indicates its photoanode only had blank TiO2 paste. The photoanode of SC-CQD was further treated by CQDs. SC-N3 and SC-CQDS-N3 are both DSSCs, the second one was treated by CQDs and N3 successively. An SEM side view of SC-TiO2 is shown in Fig. S13, the transparent TiO2 layer was 5.76 um, and the diffusive layer was 8.65 um, therefore the overall thickness of the TiO2 substrate was approximately 14.41 um. Fig. 7 shows the performance of solar cells immediately after assembly (at 0 h). Fig. 7a shows the J-V curves, and the results are summarized in Table 2 . SC-TiO2 and SC-CQD produced zero current density. The JSC and PCE of SC-N3 were 8.69 mA/cm2 and 3.63 %. After applying CQDs, SC-CQD-N3 had better performance, the JSC and PCE were 10.94 mA/cm2 and 4.25 %, increased by 25.89 % and 17.08 %. VOC and FF of SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 were close. The results were better than those of a similar study [58], when graphene quantum dots (GQDs) co-sensitized with N3, JSC and PCE increased by 17.97 % and 11.98 % respectively, reaching 5.58 mA/cm2 and 2.15 %. EQE spectra in Fig. 7b show SC-TiO2 and SC-CQD did not produce any photoelectrons, they overlapped horizontal axis. Meanwhile SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 have peak at 540 nm. According to equation (13), JSC(EQE) of SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 are 8.29 and 10.31 mA/cm2, i.e., 95.40 % and 94.24 % of their corresponding JSCs. The small discrepancies between JSCs and JSC(EQE)s validate the measurement of J-V curve and EQE spectra. Fig. 7c shows the Nyquist plots and equivalent circuit of DSSCs. From left to right, the frequencies decreased, and three semicircles can be seen. R2, R3 and R4 were obtained by fitting the left, middle and right semicircles respectively, and R1 is the intercept of the left semicircle with Z’ axis. R1 is the sheet resistance of FTO layer, R2 is the charge-transfer resistance at the electrolyte/CE interface, R3 is the resistance at the TiO2 substrate/electrolyte interface, and R4 is due to Nerst diffusion in the electrolyte [59]. Fitting results are summarized in Table S7. R1, R2, and R4 were close, because two DSSCs used the same FTO glass, counter electrode and electrolyte. However, R3 of SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 was 18.22 and 15.60 Ω. This shows CQDs enabled more facile electron transfer from electrolyte to TiO2 substrate. To study the electron lifetime, the Bode plot is also extracted (see Fig. 7d). The middle semi-circles in the Nyquist plot were at 600 – 1 Hz, within this band, two peaks on the Bode plot appear at 14.68 and 8.25 Hz. According to equation (14), the electron lifetime of SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 were 10.84 and 19.28 ms. A longer lifetime suggests the recombination of electrons in the TiO2 with oxidized electrolyte was suppressed [60]. Fig. S15 shows the long-term stability of DSSCs. After 1000 h, the PCE (see Fig. S15a) of SC-N3 and SC-CQD-N3 was 2.44 % and 3.20 %, exhibiting 67.22 % and 75.29 % of the initial values. Although previous works [29,59] achieved higher stabilities, our long-term stability curves of both PCE and JSC of SC-CQD-N3 maintained above that of SC-N3, proving the effectiveness of CQDs.

The J-V curves and EQE spectra show CQDs could not sensitize solar cells. This is because the spectral irradiance generated by solar lamp only covers 400 to 1100 nm wavelength, while the strong absorption peaks of CQDs were below 400 nm. However, CQDs were excellent co-sensitizers, and we propose two possible explanations. First, CQDs reduced the electron transfer resistance at the TiO2/electrolyte interface. This is revealed by the Nyquist plot shown in Fig. 7c, it can also be corroborated by a diagram of energy level and electron transfer route shown in Fig. 8 b. According to CV curve in Fig. 8a, the reduction and oxidation potentials were −1.38 and 1.04 V. Therefore, the LUMO and HOMO of CQDs were −3.02 and −5.44 eV, respectively (see equation (15) and (16)). The results are comparable to those in previous works [24,25,31]. The charge-transfer path is shown in Fig. 8b, the energy levels were found in previous work [24] and product website [32]. The dye electrons were excited by incident light and injected into the conduction band (CB) of the TiO2 substrate. The electrolyte iodide (I-) lost electrons to the dye and became oxidized triiodide (I3 -). The electrons entered the FTO coating and the external circuit before arriving at the Pt coating on the cathode. At the Pt-electrolyte interface, I3 - received the electrons and was reduced to I-. The LUMO and HOMO of the CQDs were between those of N3 and the CB/valence band of the TiO2 substrate. CQDs functioned as intermediaries for better energy-level alignment [24,31], facilitating electron transfer. Second, CQDs prevented the recombination of electrons, i.e., returning from CB of TiO2 substrate to the electrolyte. This is indicated by a longer electron lifetime (see Bode plot in Fig. 7d).

This study presented a revolutionary and atomic-economy-based technology for waste plastic valorization. The gaseous, liquid, and solid products of the pyrolysis–reforming of plastics serve as excellent precursors for high-value and easily marketable EP copolymers, CNFs, and CQDs. Adding oil to PAN increased the specific capacitance of the CNFs by 46.12 % to 226.64F/g, compared with that of pure PAN CNFs. With the aid of CQDs, the short-circuit current density and photo-conversion efficiency of DSSCs increased by 25.89 % and 17.08 %, respectively. The success of this study indicates that waste plastics are a valuable feedstock for manufacturing advanced materials (e.g., CNFs) and devices (e.g., solar cells) for applications in the energy transition era. This work will inspire researchers to develop revolutionary technologies for waste plastic valorization.
